text: The patient is positioned supine in lithotomy for a robotic right inguinal hernia repair using the transabdominal preperitoneal approach. Two 8mm trocars are placed in the right and left mid quadrants, with a 12mm trocar centrally positioned for the camera. The robotic system is docked between the patient's legs.

The internal surface anatomy is displayed, showing the medial umbilical ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, spermatic vessels, and vas deferens. A pantaloon-type hernia with both direct and indirect components is visible.

The surgeon begins dissection by creating a peritoneal flap starting medially at the medial umbilical ligament and extending laterally towards the anterior superior iliac spine. The dissection remains dorsal to the transversalis fascia within an avascular plane. The iliotibial tract is delineated with an arrow, and the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible. Cooper's ligament is exposed medial to the hernia sac.

The surgeon exposes the coronamortis overlying Cooper's ligament to prevent TAC injury. Cooper's ligament is clearly visible, and the dissection of the hernia sac begins. The hernia sac is reduced from the deep inguinal ring, and the spermatic cord is skeletonized using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The testicular vessels are visible to the right of the scissors and are carefully preserved along their length.

The scissors delineate the edge of the reduced peritoneal sac. The surgeon continues dissecting the peritoneum from its retroperitoneal structures to prepare for mesh placement. Tacks are placed medially on Cooper's ligament and laterally above the iliopubic tract to secure the mesh, avoiding the triangle of doom and triangle of pain. The eight millimeter trocar ipsilateral to the hernia defect is undocked, and the assistant deploys the mesh into the inguinal space. Additional tacks are placed on Cooper's ligament and in the supralateral position, followed by closure of the peritoneal flap using the attacking device.
